skill.name: azure-storage-file-share-ts
skill.description: Guides JavaScript and TypeScript developers through Azure File Share operations with @azure/storage-file-share, including authentication, shares, directories, file transfers, metadata, snapshots, and SAS tokens.
skill.summary: Use the Azure File Share SDK from JavaScript or TypeScript to manage SMB shares, directories, files, metadata, and SAS access.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, network, filesystem, environment, sensitive, weak crypto, and heuristic findings. Review found these are Markdown code examples for the Azure Storage File Share SDK, with no executable helper scripts, no prompt injection text, and no evidence of credential exfiltration. The skill should publish with a medium warning because it teaches handling storage keys, SAS tokens, local file transfer, network operations, and destructive delete APIs.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Azure Credential Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The guide shows environment variables for Azure storage account keys, connection strings, and SAS tokens. This is legitimate SDK documentation, but users could expose powerful credentials if they log, commit, or over-scope these values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly use Azure storage secrets and SAS tokens. The usage is instructional rather than malicious, so the risk is operational credential exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Cloud File Transfer and Destructive Operation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents uploading local files, streaming local file contents, downloading files to disk, deleting shares, deleting directories, deleting files, deleting snapshots, and copying from a URL. These are expected Azure Files capabilities, but they can modify local and remote data if copied into production without safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly cover local file access and destructive Azure File Share APIs. They are normal SDK operations, but the impact is material if users run them against real shares.

medium_findings.title: Broad SAS Permission Example
medium_findings.description: The share SAS example grants read, create, write, delete, and list permissions for 24 hours. This can be appropriate for administration, but marketplace users should treat it as elevated access and prefer least privilege.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission string and expiration are directly visible in the SDK example. It is not malicious, but it demonstrates a broad capability set that should be constrained in real systems.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings are from Markdown examples and code fences, including an npm install command. No standalone script, runtime hook, or hidden command execution path was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only reviewed file is SKILL.md, and the flagged lines are documentation examples. There is no evidence of executable skill code or command construction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings align with words inside documentation such as description, destination, and SDK sample text, not use of DES, MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not implement cryptography. They are front matter or file operation examples, so the scanner appears to have matched substrings rather than crypto APIs.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint URL Examples Are Expected Network Usage
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Azure Files service endpoints or illustrative source file URLs. They do not point to unknown collection services, paste sites, or unrelated external hosts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match the stated purpose of using Azure File Share APIs. No evidence found that credentials or local files are sent to an unrelated third-party endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Access in SDK Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read Azure connection strings, account keys, account names, and SAS tokens from process.env. This is conventional configuration, but the values are sensitive and must not be exposed in prompts, logs, or repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names are explicit and include secret-bearing Azure credentials. The examples do not exfiltrate them, but they do teach handling secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Reads and Writes in SDK Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read local file metadata, create read streams, upload local paths, and download remote files to a local destination. Users should ensure paths are trusted and avoid applying these snippets to sensitive local files without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem calls are concrete and tied to upload and download operations. They are legitimate examples, but they can move local data across the network.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Delete and Snapshot Removal Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes delete methods for shares, directories, files, and snapshots. These are normal management operations, but they are destructive and should be guarded by confirmation, backups, and least-privilege credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples call delete methods directly. The context is instructional, so the risk depends on how users adapt the snippets.

content.user_title: Manage Azure File Shares with TypeScript
content.value_statement: Azure File Share workflows require precise SDK calls for authentication, files, directories, metadata, and SAS access. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused examples for building those operations in JavaScript or TypeScript.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install @azure/storage-file-share and @azure/identity for Node.js projects.
Explains connection string, shared key, DefaultAzureCredential, and SAS token authentication patterns.
Provides examples for creating, listing, configuring, and deleting Azure file shares.
Covers directory and file operations, including upload, download, streaming, ranges, and copy operations.
Documents metadata, HTTP headers, snapshots, SAS token generation, and common TypeScript imports.
Summarizes Node.js and browser support differences for key SDK features.
limitations: It is a reference guide, not an executable library or validation tool.
Examples use placeholder paths, share names, account names, and credentials that must be replaced safely.
It does not cover Azure portal setup, IAM policy design, private networking, or full production hardening.
Several examples can delete or overwrite data if copied without environment checks.

use_cases.title: Build File Share Automation
use_cases.description: Create TypeScript services that upload, download, list, and manage files in Azure File Shares.

use_cases.title: Prepare Operations Scripts
use_cases.description: Draft Node.js maintenance scripts for shares, directories, quotas, snapshots, and metadata updates.

use_cases.title: Review SDK Integration Choices
use_cases.description: Compare authentication options and platform support before selecting a browser or Node.js implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show a minimal TypeScript setup for connecting to Azure File Share with DefaultAzureCredential. Include required packages and environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Upload and Download Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft TypeScript code that uploads a local file to an Azure File Share, then downloads it to a controlled destination path.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Shares and Directories
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an idempotent maintenance script that creates a share, creates nested directories, lists files, and handles expected Azure errors.

prompt_templates.title: Design Secure SAS Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose a least-privilege SAS strategy for one file and one share. Explain permissions, expiry, and secret handling safeguards.

output_examples.input: Create a TypeScript plan for uploading large report files to Azure Files.
output: A package and authentication checklist for @azure/storage-file-share.
A streaming upload approach with file size checks and controlled local paths.
Operational notes for quotas, retries, and avoiding accidental overwrites.

output_examples.input: Explain which Azure File Share authentication method to use in production.
output_examples.output: A comparison of connection strings, shared keys, managed identity, and SAS tokens, with a recommendation to prefer DefaultAzureCredential when the workload supports it.

output_examples.input: Generate guidance for safe cleanup of old files in a share.
output: A deletion workflow that lists candidates before removal.
Checks for snapshots, backups, and least-privilege credentials.
Error handling guidance for missing files, access denial, and conflicts.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential or scoped SAS tokens over long-lived account keys when possible.
Validate local file paths, share names, and directory names before running upload or download code.
Add confirmations, backups, and dry-run modes around delete and overwrite operations.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real connection strings, account keys, or SAS tokens into prompts or source code.
Do not grant broad SAS permissions when read-only or short-lived access is sufficient.
Do not run delete examples against production shares without explicit safeguards.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands directly?
faq.answer: No. It is Markdown guidance with SDK examples. Users must run adapted code in their own project.

faq.question: Which SDK package does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on @azure/storage-file-share for JavaScript and TypeScript Azure File Share operations.

faq.question: Can it help with managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes DefaultAzureCredential examples and notes that managed identity is preferred for production where supported.

faq.question: Does it cover browser usage?
faq.answer: Partly. It lists platform differences and shows that several Node.js features are not available in browsers.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with real storage credentials?
faq.answer: Use caution. Keep credentials out of prompts, logs, and repositories, and prefer least-privilege access.

faq.question: Can examples delete data?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes delete operations for shares, directories, files, and snapshots, so add safeguards before use.
